coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation of the multi-layer wetting behavior of electrode-separator-composites in lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kaden, Nicolaj
Nguyen, Do M.
Schlimbach, Ricarda
Ohnimus, Tobias
Kwade, Arno
Dröder, Klaus
coredata.dc:description: The success of lithium-ion batteries and their application in electromobility is highly dependent on their scalable and cost-efficient manufacturing. Herein, electrolyte filling and wetting are time-consuming and quality-critical process steps, which have been, on the material level, primarily researched on an intra-material level for single-cell components while lacking overarching investigations for inter-material effects during wetting. This study demonstrates for the first time an experimental methodology designed to systematically quantify the interaction effects between different electrode-separator-composites during wetting by varying product components and process parameters. The investigation reveals a quantifiable interaction within a two- or three-layer material combination of anode, separator, and cathode, which can be discerned into distinct anode and cathode interaction effects, maintaining consistency across the product and parameter variations. Through the analysis of activation energy using the Arrhenius equation, the study observe a combinatorial relationship for modeling multi-layer wetting. Multi-layer wetting rates increase by 215 % compared to single material rates. Higher temperatures boost penetration rates due to lower viscosity, but reduce the interaction effect. These insights contribute to an application-orientated understanding of electrode-separator-composite wetting behavior and open up more possibilities for an optimized manufacturing process through expanding methodical exploration of strategies for counterbalancing poor wettability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrolyte wetting
Tensiometry
Multi-layer wetting
Inter-material wetting
Lithium-ion battery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of the inter-material and interfacial interaction effects (TS1)
Variation of the process parameter “electrolyte temperature” (TS2)
Variation of the product parameter “cathode material” (TS5)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the support by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) for funding this research within the research project Cell-Fill (grant number 03XP0237A). Cell-Fill investigates different parameters influencing wetting, innovative filling processes and measurement methods, as well as simulation models for dispensing and wetting. A special thanks goes to S\u00F6ren Scheffler for the contribution of the different calendered cathode material and the SEM-images of these cathodes. This material was produced in the project InteKal (grant number 03XP0348A) funded by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) for the InZePro competence cluster. We acknowledge support by the Open Access Publication Funds of Technische Universit\u00E4t Braunschweig.

We are grateful for the support by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) for funding this research within the research project Cell-Fill (grant number 03XP0237A). Cell-Fill investigates different parameters influencing wetting, innovative filling processes and measurement methods, as well as simulation models for dispensing and wetting. A special thanks goes to S\u00F6ren Scheffler for the contribution of the different calendered cathode material and the SEM-images of these cathodes. This material was produced in the project InteKal (grant number 03XP0348A) funded by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) for the InZePro competence cluster.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the intra-material, inter-material and interfacial wetting paths in an electrode-separator composite (ESC) according to Peters et al. [27] (a) as well as the entry paths of the electrolyte into the ESC from the dead volume (b) and an overview of the studies available in the literature on the factors influencing the wetting behavior at the material level (c). The colored fields represent the research gaps investigated in this study.
Illustration of the design of the test equipment used (a) tensiometer (b) holder during measurement, (c) evaluation methodology, and (d) wetting balance test results.

Experimental design of the penetration rate measurements for the investigation of the interaction effect between selected ESC components with preliminary tests to measure intra-material wetting and identify holder influence (Row I) and main tests (Rows II, III, and IV). The two column blocks (X,Y,Z and x,y,z) represent the designs of the compared samples through which the influences were identified. The "&" character indicates components that are measured together, whereas the "+" character indicates superposed components.

Penetration rates of the multi-layer wetting tests (Row IV in Fig. 3) for the three-layer combination measurements (blue), the superpositions of the individual measurements (red and yellow) and the calculated interaction effects of the multi-layer wetting (green). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Penetration rates of the multi-layer wetting tests for the temperature variation TS2 of 20, 40 and 60 °C [a] and their calculated interaction effects for the two- and three-layer measurements [b]. The penetration rates of the multi-layer wetting tests for the separator material variation TS3 [c] and their calculated interaction effects for the two- and three-layer measurements [d]. The penetration rates of the multi-layer wetting tests with NMC622 calendering variation TS5 of 2.5 g cm−3 (C2), 2.9 g cm−3 (C3) and 3.2 g cm−3 (C4) [e] and their calculated interaction effects for the two- and three-layer measurements [f]. The penetration rates of the multi-layer wetting tests for the cathode material variation TS4 of NMC622 (C3) and NMC811 (C5) both with 2.9 g cm−3 [g] and their calculated interaction effects for the two- and three-layer measurements [h].

The measured wetting rates D of the temperature variation plotted against the inverse of the temperature with the fit of the regression line and the resulting activation energy Eα,measured for anode, cathode, and separator.
Penetration rates and wetting rates of the intra-material separator measurements in dependence of their porosity.
SEM images of the electrode surface of cathode C2 with a density of 2.5 g cm−3 (a) C3 with a density of 2.9 g cm−3 (b) and C4 with a density of 3.2 g cm−3 (c).
Overview of the electrode materials used.
Overview of the separator materials used.
Description of the performed test series in relation to the varied parameters.

row.entry: S2 to S5 and lamination of S5 to the electrodes at roller temperature of 100 °C, contact pressure of 5.5 N mm−2 and feed of 1 m min−1 on the lamination system SDL 50 (Polatek SL-Laminiertechnik GmbH, Germany)
Uncalendered NMC622 cathodes 2.5 g cm−3 (C2) and with a calendering level of 2.9 g cm−3 (C3) and 3.2 g cm−3 (C4)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the measured activation energies Eα,measured of the intra-material, two-layer and three-layer measurements (top) and the calculated activation energy Eα,fit from the functional relationship f (Eα1,Eα2) = D1 (Eα1∙Eα2)/(Eα1+Eα2) + D2.
Key results of the performed test series in relation to the varied parameters.

row.entry: Quantification of interaction effect I in multi-layer wetting, validated through control sample and Eq. (14). Three-layer measurement accelerates wetting by at least 215 % compared to intra-material penetration rates.

The penetration rate increases with rising temperature, following the Arrhenius relationship, while the interaction effect decreases. Description of the activation energies of the multilayer combinations by functional correlation

Intra-material wetting differences of separators are mitigated in multi-layer wetting, reducing the wetting bottleneck's criticality. Lamination in this material combination deteriorates wetting behavior; PVDF application method significantly influences wetting behavior.
NMC811 material shows a significant higher interaction effect than NMC622.
Penetration rate decrease with increasing calendering density for single and multi-layer setups. Interaction effect is higher for calendered cathodes

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation of the multi-layer wetting behavior of electrode-separator-composites in lithium-ion batteries

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Machine Tools and Production Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The success of lithium-ion batteries and their application in electromobility is highly dependent on their scalable and cost-efficient manufacturing. Herein, electrolyte filling and wetting are time-consuming and quality-critical process steps, which have been, on the material level, primarily researched on an intra-material level for single-cell components while lacking overarching investigations for inter-material effects during wetting. This study demonstrates for the first time an experimental methodology designed to systematically quantify the interaction effects between different electrode-separator-composites during wetting by varying product components and process parameters. The investigation reveals a quantifiable interaction within a two- or three-layer material combination of anode, separator, and cathode, which can be discerned into distinct anode and cathode interaction effects, maintaining consistency across the product and parameter variations. Through the analysis of activation energy using the Arrhenius equation, the study observe a combinatorial relationship for modeling multi-layer wetting. Multi-layer wetting rates increase by 215 % compared to single material rates. Higher temperatures boost penetration rates due to lower viscosity, but reduce the interaction effect. These insights contribute to an application-orientated understanding of electrode-separator-composite wetting behavior and open up more possibilities for an optimized manufacturing process through expanding methodical exploration of strategies for counterbalancing poor wettability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to reduce CO2 emissions in the automotive sector, the pursuit of carbon-neutral mobility has become paramount, driven by increasingly stringent political regulations [1,2]. A pivotal technology to achieve short and medium-term sustainability goals, particularly in automotive applications, is the utilization of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) [3,4]. These batteries are known for advantageous properties such as a high energy density, long cycle life, and scalable manufacturability while being a viable solution for reducing mobility-based carbon emissions [5]. However, in order to allow for a widespread adoption of LIBs and to meet the likely rising demands of the future, enhancing productivity within the production processes becomes essential [6].

Next to material expenses, production costs stand out as the lever for cost savings and increased throughput [7]. The production of LIBs consists of the production of electrodes, their assembly into cells together with other components, and the conditioning of these cells through formation and aging where production costs are divided approximately equally [8,9]. Within the production steps of cell manufacturing, the electrolyte filling process emerges as a key area for potential improvement [9].

The electrolyte filling process involves the dispersion of electrolyte into the cell and subsequent wetting of electrode-separator composites (ESC) pores [10]. This step, while crucial, poses challenges in scalability due to required wetting times, necessitating appropriate storage capacities [7]. The duration of wetting is a critical factor influencing battery quality, as insufficient wetting may lead to inhomogeneous current distributions during formation. This acts as a catalyst for dendrite growth - possible spots for lithium plating - and results in performance losses [11,12]. Kaden et al.'s study highlights the existing research gaps within the scientific exploration of the electrolyte filling process. These gaps include the absence of a clear guiding measurement method, insufficient consideration of mutual influences, and a limited exploration of scaling wetting behavior to large-format battery cells [10].

At the material level, previous studies have primarily focused on intra-material wetting, investigating individual materials independently. This approach is evident in studies involving material modifications, new recipes for electrodes or electrolytes, and variations in process parameters [13–26]. However, a crucial step towards scaling material wetting requires a comprehension of wetting characteristics for combination of materials that have not yet been sufficiently investigated, especially when electrode and separator materials are wetted as a composite.

Fig. 1 illustrates distinct wetting paths for ESCs, each elucidated in the existing literature. The principal route for electrolyte flow is located at the cross-sectional area at the edges of the ESC, originating from the liquid reservoir within the cell housing's dead volume. Subsequently, the electrolyte permeates the pore systems of the individual single materials, moving along intra-material wetting paths denoted as paths 1 and 2. Notably, a narrow gap at the contact surfaces between the electrodes and the separator introduces an additional path for interfacial wetting beyond the dead volume, identified as path 3 [27]. The geometric dimensions of narrow gaps depend on the product parameters of the packaging and encapsulation processes. For instance, in wound and z-folded packages, the winding tension determines the compression of the ESCs and thus the narrow gap between the layers [28,29]. The cell's differential pressure between the inside and outside in soft cases and the ratio of package stack thickness to the internal dimensions of the hard case are further factors that influence the compression [30,31]. Processes such as lamination or hot pressing of ESCs also affect the interface [32]. Despite its size in relation to the cross-sectional areas of the cell composite edges, this narrow gap serves as a crucial conduit for electrolyte exchange between the materials, establishing inter-material wetting paths (path 4). The exploration of these inter-material and interfacial wetting paths, which is the focus of this study, holds the potential to alleviate bottlenecks associated with intra-material wetting. As such, understanding and optimizing these intricate wetting pathways are imperative for enhancing the overall efficiency and quality of LIB production [27].

The exploration of inter-material and interfacial wetting has so far been only addressed through experimental means to a limited extent. Beyer et al. in a systematic study, measured the wetting properties of electrodes and various separators, including their combinations. Their findings highlighted qualitative shifts in wetting behavior when multiple materials are combined. However, the quantitative evaluation of inter-material wetting effects was notably absent [33]. In a related publication, Wanner and Birke conducted experimental studies on inter-material wetting between anodes and separators, serving as validation tests for their simulation model. Their research revealed a remarkable increase in wetting rate – approximately 43 % – attributed to additional wetting in the interface [34]. While their study provided qualitative insights, a comprehensive quantitative analysis remained elusive. Beyer et al. used a gravimetric measuring method known as the wetting balance test, implemented with a tensiometer [33]. This method, which was first introduced by Kühnel et al. involves vertically immersing the material edge into a liquid, with the subsequent measurement of the temporal increase in weight over time. The method's high resolution rendered it particularly effective [35]. Similarly, Wanner and Birke applied a comparable structure, employing optical measurement of the temporal change in the propagation front through the capillary rise test [36]. The limitation of the optical capillary rise test as a measuring device for multi-layer wetting tests lies in a two-material combination of electrode and separator, as no conclusions can be drawn from opaque electrodes. Optical measurement of ESC wetting via the cut edge is also difficult due to the very low layer thickness in relation to the other geometric dimensions and can possibly be falsified due to edge effects. Additionally, other well-known tests, such as the droplet spread test and a variant of the wetting balance test, were employed to measure electrolyte absorption by weighing a sample at different times [37].

While the wetting of material combinations has often been a subject of simulation studies using Lattice-Boltzmann models, existing research primarily focuses on factors modifying the pore morphology of electrodes rather than the transition from intra-material wetting to composite wetting [38–41]. Lee et al. Wanner and Birke, and Jeon et al. simulated electrolyte spread in a two-layer composite comprising a separator and either an anode or cathode [34,42,43]. Shodiev et al. in contrast, examined a three-layer composite in their simulation studies on cell stacking materials [44]. However, the emphasis in these studies lay on understanding influencing factors altering electrode pore morphology or the flow direction of the electrolyte was assumed to be orthogonal to the interface surface.

In light of these gaps, the approach of this study aims towards a systematic investigation and quantification of multi-layer wetting (inter-material and interfacial wetting) by implementing a new parameter called interaction effect. This study introduce an experimental method to quantify these interaction effects between the different ESC materials and, through its application, systematically explore various product and process parameter variations, such as temperature, degree of calendering and different cathode and separator materials. This methodology aims to operationalize the influence of these parameters on multi-layer wetting behavior, offering a more comprehensive understanding for improving the scalability of LIB production which has been thus far underexplored in the scientific literature. By knowing how to scale up single-layer wetting to multilayer wetting, more quantifiable estimates of wetting behavior at the cell level can be made and potential material- and cost-intensive test series can be reduced.

The remainder of this paper is outlined as follows: Section 2 describes the experimental setup, materials used and the measurement method to identify the interaction effect. Then, the Results and Discussion in section 3 presents the key findings from the experiments, including the first quantification of inter-material and interfacial wetting effects. Finally, the Conclusion and Outlook section summarizes the main outcomes of the research and outlines potential directions for future studies.

The different materials used are presented in the following section. This is followed by a description of the experimental setup, its extensions for measuring the interaction effect and the evaluation methodology for determining the wetting parameters. Finally, the novel experimental methodology and the measurement design are theoretically introduced and explained.

A variety of electrodes and separators is utilized in the experimental investigations, detailing their properties and assignment to specific test series in Tables 1 and 2
. While one set of calendered anodes (A1) and cathodes (C1) were supplied by CustomCells, the remaining cathode types C2 to C5 were supplied by Targray and calendered by the Institute for Particle Technology (iPAT) in the Battery LabFactory Braunschweig (BLB). The anode A1 is composed of 94 % SMGMK1C graphite, 2 % carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), 3 % styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR), and 1 % carbon black Super C65. Conversely, cathode C1 is composed of 95.5 % cathode active material
L i
N i
0.6
C
o 0.2
M
n 0.2
O 2
(NMC622), 3 % conductive additive, and 1.5 % of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) as a binder. The remaining cathodes C2 to C4 also feature 95.5 % NMC622 (BASF), while cathode C5 incorporates
L i
N i
0.8
C
o 0.1
M
n 0.1
O 2
(NMC811, BASF). All cathodes share a binder content of 1.5 % PVDF (Solef 5130, Solvay), with 2.25 % carbon black (C-NERGY™ Super C65, Imerys) and 0.75 % conductive graphite (C-NERGY™ SFG6L, Imerys) as conductive agents. All electrodes feature a double-sided coating with the details given in Table 1. While for the anode a copper current collector is used, on the cathode side the standard material for the current collector is aluminum. C3 to C5 were calendered to the respective target density using a two-roll calendar (GLK400, Saueressig).

Distinct separators, crucial for outlining the electrochemical interactions between the anode and different cathodes, are sourced from various companies as specified in Table 2. These separators were selected on the basis of individual characteristics to ensure a comprehensive exploration of multi-layer wetting behavior. The experiments employ the electrolyte LP572 from PuriEL, with a composition of 1 M lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) in 30 wt% ethylene carbonate (EC) and 70 wt% ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC). Additionally, 2 wt% vinylene carbonate (VC) are included as an additive to enhance the electrolyte's performance. This electrolyte composition was specifically selected for its compatibility with the selected electrodes and separators, ensuring a reliable and consistent experimental setup.

To assess wetting properties, samples from all test series are subjected to measurements using a wetting balance test with a DCAT25 tensiometer (Dataphysics Instruments GmbH, Germany). Fig. 2 a illustrates the used tensiometer set-up, adapted for ESC materials. A custom-made holder, replacing the supplied standard foil holder FO 11, enables the measurement of multi-layer samples, as it presses the samples through two transparent polyethylene (PE) plates (20 × 36 mm2), creating a uniform interface between the sample surfaces. For this purpose, six round disc magnet pairs S-08-03-N52N (Webcraft GmbH, Germany) and a distance of 1.4 mm between the magnets were chosen without taking the sample thickness into account in order to ensure uniform pressure. The placement of the magnet pair can be seen in Fig. 2 b. The distribution of the resulting pressure through the PE plates on the measurement samples was determined in a simulation (Ansys Mechanical) and the utilization of the pressure measurement film PreScale 4LW (FUJIFILM Europe GmbH, Germany). The distribution can be assumed to be approximately uniform over the plate surface and is on average between 420 and 490 mbar depending on the thickness of the materials (see Table S1 Supplementary Material). This pressure is lower than the differential pressure present in soft case batteries, which is 900–950 mbar depending on the evacuation pressure. This falls within a spectrum that aligns with the practical requirements of the given application [18,19]. A glass dish, acting as a liquid container, features a cover with a passage for samples, minimizing liquid evaporation during experiments.

For details on the measurement process, evaluation, and derivation of relevant wetting parameters – penetration rate K [g∙s−0.5] and wetting rate D [mm∙s−0.5] – refer to the gravimetric method described in Kaden et al.'s publication [45]. The penetration rate K is derived directly from the measurement of the mass m, while the wetting rate D in this measurement setup represents the mathematical normalization of the mass m to the wettable sample thickness d and the porosity ε. Deviating from the mentioned procedure, the chosen measurement duration is set to 420 s, with the evaluation range for determining penetration rate K set between 8 and 18 s0.5, and ten measurements being taken per sample point (see Fig. 2c and d). Shear cutting on a Fiskar ProCision paper cutting machine is performed with samples prepared to a size of 20 x 40 mm2. The experiments are conducted under constant conditions in a dry room of the BLB (dew point of −40 °C, ambient temperature of 20 °C).

Temperature control of the electrolyte is ensured through the peltier temperature control unit of the tensiometer, maintaining a constant temperature of 20 °C throughout all experiments except the temperature variations. To further assess temperature-dependent wetting properties, considering the interaction between ESC materials and the electrolyte, additional evaluations employ the methodology proposed by Davoadbaadi et al. [20]. This method facilitates the determination of activation energy from the Arrhenius equation:
(1)
D = A ⋅
e
−
E α
R T
(2)
ln
( D )
= ln
( B )
−
(
E α
R
)
⋅
1 T
where R is the ideal gas constant [kJ∙(mol∙K)−1], E
α is the activation energy [kJ∙mol−1], B is the pre-exponential factor [mm∙s−0.5], T is the temperature [K] and D is the wetting rate [mm∙s−0.5].

The activation energy serves as an energetic barrier, which must be overcome by the driving capillary forces, representing the sensitivity with which the ESC-electrolyte combination responds to temperature changes. Lower activation energies correspond to heightened wetting rates at consistent temperatures or a diminished increase in wetting rate with temperature fluctuations.

Considering statistical relevance of the results, normal distribution of all samples is verified through the Shapiro-Wilk test, known for its robustness with sample sizes below 50 [46]. Levene's test shows homogeneity of variances. Depending on the prerequisites established for normally distributed samples, either t-test (homogeneous variance) or the Welch's t-test (heterogeneous variance) are employed for further analysis. These rigorous statistical assessments ensure an overall robustness and reliability.

In order to examine diverse product and process influences on the wetting behavior of multi-layer samples, five distinct test series of penetration rate measurements were conducted. The initial test series (TS1) concentrates on quantifying inter-material and interfacial interaction effects which occur during measurements of at least two layers, and represents the validation of the multilayer measurement methodology, with the procedural details illustrated in Fig. 3 . 1
1 The quantity referred to as multi-layer wetting includes the wetting processes of the inter-material wetting between at least two materials as well as the interfacial wetting along the material surfaces. Multi-layer wetting occurs in both the two- and three-layer combination measurements. Commencing the experiments, intra-material wetting measurements for individual materials (A1, C1, and S1) as monolayer samples are conducted (Row I, Fig. 3). Subsequently, combinations of anode, cathode, and separator in both two-layer (Row II, Fig. 3) and three-layer structures (Row III and IV, Fig. 3) undergo systematic measurements and comparisons to evaluate the interaction effects caused by the additional wetting paths. An additional evaluation consider the measuring holder as a potential influencing factor. The respective methodology (changes in the tests) for identifying the different interaction effects and setup influences are shown in the two left and center column, each consisting of three configurations. While the left column shows the setup for the respective measurement (capital letters X,Y,Z), the middle column (lowercase letters x,y,z) presents the composition of the control variable against which the measurements will be compared. These controls are characterized either by a different holder (Row I) or by the calculated superposition of measurements from configurations with less (1 or 2) layers. The resulting, calculated values of the wetting rate allow for the determination of interaction effects for two- or three-layer combinations. This either involves subtracting the measured value of the two- or three-layer measurements (Rows II or III) from the superposition of intra-material measurements (Row I) or the three-layer measurements (Row IV) from the superposition of the two-layer measurements (Row II) and the intra-material separator measurement (Row I). The resulting wetting difference between the experimental and control measurements represents the interaction effect I [g∙s−0.5], comprising both inter-material and interfacial wetting. Further, this study denotes penetration rate measurements involving multiple components with an "&" character, while marking superpositions of measurement results with a "+" character. The letters employed here represent the indices for penetration rates. To enhance clarity, the formula symbol K for penetration rate has been omitted for better visualization (A&A&S = K
A&A&S ). This systematic approach allows for a nuanced understanding of the interplay between different components in the wetting process.

For the evaluation of the holder influence, both the standard FO 11 foil holder (I.x, I.y, I.z) and the custom-made holder (I.X, I.Y, I.Z) were used. The two- and three-layer combinations adopt a layer structure with electrodes positioned externally, ensuring uniformity across all experiments using the custom-made holder (Rows II, III, IV). This careful experimental design aims to discern and quantify the intricate influences of the different combinations on multi-layer wetting, providing a robust foundation for subsequent analyses.

For the two-layer combinations (Row II), the interactions effect I
A,A , I
C,C , and I
A,C between both electrodes can be calculated for the two-layer combinations from Eqs. (3)–(5).These interactions effects describe the change in wetting through the gap between the electrodes and through the exchange between the electrode surfaces.
(3)
I

= A & A −

)
(4)
I

= C & C −

(5)

= A & C −

)

The interaction effects I
A , I
C , and I
AC (Row III), which are calculated according to Eqs. (6)–(8), correspond to the multilayer wetting of the three-layer combination in relation to the superposition of the intra-material wettings. They show the change in wetting caused by the two interfaces and by the inter-material exchange of the separator with the two electrodes.
(6)

Further interaction effects I
A,S,A , I
C,S,C and I
A,S,C are calculated from the change in wetting of the two-layer combinations to the three-layer combinations. Here, three-layer combinations (Row IV) are compared with the superposition of the two-layer combination and the separator. This isolates the influence of the separator as an additional layer with its own inter-material and interfacial wetting path.
(9)

)

)

While I
A and I
C correspond to the interaction effect at the two interfaces between the similar electrodes (anode or cathode) and the separator, the parameter I
AC represents the interaction effect at the interfaces between the separator and the two different electrodes. Due to the symmetrical structure and the assumption of an even distribution across both interfaces in the three-layer combinations with two identical electrodes, it is assumed, that the inter-material wetting of one electrode layer and hence the interaction effect with two different electrodes can be calculated as follows:
(12)
I

= ½ ∙

+
I
A , A
)
(13)
I
C / 2
= ½ ∙
I C
= ½ ∙
(
I
C , S , C
+
I
C , C
)
(14)
I
A C
=
I
A / 2
+
I
C / 2
This connection overdetermines the system of equations, allowing for the validation of the postulated connection through one of the measurements.

The standard deviation of the interaction effect parameters is calculated using the error propagation law Eq. (15), accounting for errors assumed to be independent of each other.
(15)
δ q =
(
∂ q
∂
x 1
δ
x 1
)
2
+ … +
(
∂ q
∂
x n
δ
x n
)
2

For better accessibility of the results, all findings in this paper are presented with the penetration rate K [g∙s−0.5] as an absolute statement of the additional measured electrolyte mass m absorbed over time t. In the TS1 and TS2 test series in particular, all results refer to the same material combinations, considering identical layer thicknesses d and porosity ε. Furthermore, the wetting rate D [mm∙s−0.5] is calculated for each set of results. This enables a normalized comparison across material variations, as observed in series TS3 – TS5.
The subsequent test series systematically vary different process and product parameters within this test system to assess their impacts on multi-layer wetting (see Table 3 ).

From the literature, temperature is recognized as one of the most significant factors influencing improved wetting speed [18,20,27]. Consequently, this process parameter is varied within the common operating temperature range up to 60 °C. According to the literature, separators, particularly the most commonly used polyolefin separators, exhibit poor intra-material wetting properties [47]. Therefore, varying these product properties is of particular interest, as it may demonstrate the extent to which multi-layer wetting can mitigate this bottleneck.

The process in electrode production defining the final density and porosity of the electrode and thereby having a high influence on the electrode wetting behavior as well as energy and power density – namely calendering. To cover a relevant range of calendering degrees and at the same time introduce measurable differences in wetting behaviour, three different target densities were chosen, starting with the uncalendered electrode and ending with an industrially relevant density of 3.2 g cm−3

Based on the calendering investigations of the cathode this work compares two different cathode active materials for the sake of doing the transfer from the readily available cathode consisting of NMC622 to a more industry-relevant cathode consisting of NMC811. Here the goal is to prove the applicability of the proposed analysis method to various active materials including industrially relevant NMC811.

The conducted experiments determine the influence of the custom-made holder through the interfacial paths in the gap between the electrodes or separator surface and the holder surface on the results (Row I). There are no significant differences between the penetration rates measured at the anode (p = 0.9505) and the cathode (p = 0.9819) available with the custom-made holder (I.X, I.Y) and standard FO 11 foil holder (I.x, I.y). For the separator, the mean values for experiments with the custom-made holder (I.Z) and standard FO 11 foil holder (I.z) significantly differ from each other (p < 0.001) (see Table S2 Supplementary Material).

Interpreting these results, it becomes evident that the contact between the separator and the holder surface enhances the penetration rate, unlike the electrode measurements. This increase in penetration rate for the separator could be attributed to the absence of an impenetrable arrester foil layer, which is present in electrodes coated on both sides. Additionally, the separator's thinner and more compressible nature might cause greater capillary forces due to the gap between the plates. Another plausible explanation could be the material similarity between the holder (polyethylene) and the separator (polyester), potentially resulting in a lower contact angle at the interface with the electrolyte compared to the three-phase separator/electrolyte/air limit. In two- and three-layer combinations (Rows II and III), one electrode surface is always in contact with the holder, but no significant differences in penetration rates were detected for the electrodes, indicating this contact does not influence the measurements for these setups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of the inter-material and interfacial interaction effects (TS1)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 illustrates the results derived from measurements aimed at quantifying inter-material and interfacial interaction effects. The three blocks, progressing from left to right, present the results for the two- and three-layer measurements of the anode-separator (Block X), cathode-separator (Block Y), and anode-cathode-separator combination (Block Z).

In each block from left to right, the bars have the following meaning.
•
Left stacked bar: resulting measurement from the superposition of intra-material measurement of the two electrodes (yellow/ochre, II.x).
•
Next bar: calculated interaction effects IA,A of the two-layer combination (green), encompassing inter-material and interfacial wetting paths along the electrode-electrode interface.
•
Next stack bar: superposition of the two-layer combination (red, II.X) and the intra-material measurement of the separator (yellow/ochre, I.Z).
•
Next to it: calculated interaction effects of the separator addition IA,S,A and the three-layer combination IA (green), encompassing inter-material and interfacial wetting paths along the electrode-separator interface.
•
Right side of the block: results of the three-layer combination measurements (blue, III.X).

The combination of materials, from single to two-layer and three-layer combinations, induces an increase in penetration rate compared to the sum of the individual measurements, indicating a positive influence of inter-material and interfacial exchange. Statistically significant differences in mean penetration rates were observed for two-layer combinations of electrodes (II.X, II.Y, II.Z) compared to the sum of intra-material electrode measurements (II.x, II.y, II.z) (p < 0.001). This significant difference (p < 0.001) also appears for three-layer combinations (III.X, III.Y, III.Z) compared to their intra-material superpositions (III.x, III.Y, III.z) and the superpositions of the two-layer combination with the intra-material separator. The three-layer combinations show an increase in penetration rate by 215 %, 248 %, and 231 % for A&S&A, C&S&C, and A&S&C, respectively, compared to the superposition of the penetration rates of the respective intra-material measurements.

These findings show, that it is evident that inter-material and interfacial wetting paths enable slower-wettable materials to experience additional wetting from faster-wettable materials via the contact surface, orthogonally to intra-material wetting. This mechanism can alleviate material-related wetting bottlenecks. The ratio of the interaction effects of two-layer to three-layer combinations shows that the ratio I
C,C /I
C (80.61 %) for the cathode-separator combination is approximately 20 percentage points higher than the ratio I
A,A /I
A (59.23 %) for the anode-separator combination.

This interaction effect is highlighted in Block Z, which shows the magnitude of the interaction effect associated with the A&C&S combination compared to the other interaction effects in Blocks X and Y. The experimentally determined values I
A , I
C , I
AC and the other interaction effects groups align with Eqs. (12)–(14), confirming the validity of a uniform distribution of multi-layer wetting for symmetrical combinations. Although the mean comparison results detailed in Tables S3–S5 in the Supplementary Material show high variances due to error propagation, the overall trends validate the influence of inter-material and interfacial interactions on the wetting behavior in multi-layer setups.

In summary, the interaction effect in the form of the characteristic value I for multi-layer wetting is quantified using the measurement and evaluation methodology of this study. The control sample of the characteristic value for the two electrodes through an additional measurement and Eq. (14) validates this methodology. The interaction effect of a three-layer measurement accelerates wetting by at least 215 % compared to intra-material penetration rates. Thus, in practice, material-related bottlenecks and suitable material pairings can be identified and quantified cost- and time-efficiently through measurements at the material level and subsequently applied to the cell level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of the process parameter “electrolyte temperature” (TS2)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 depicts the penetration rates for the test series TS2 – TS5 and the associated interaction effects. The corresponding figure illustrating the wetting rates is shown in the Supplementary Material under Fig. S6.

The penetration rate of the separator, along with all combinations of two and three layers, is depicted in the initial row of Fig. 5 measured at electrolyte temperatures of 20, 40, and 60 °C (a). This is accompanied by the corresponding sizes of inter-material and interfacial interaction effects from the two- and three-layer measurements (b). An increase in penetration rates for all measurement combinations is evident with rising temperature, particularly the significant rise in the penetration rate of the separator, which at 60 °C is approximately within the same order of magnitude as the two-layer measurements.

The escalation in penetration rates is especially noticeable in setups with fewer layers. At 60 °C, the penetration rates measured at different points show no significant differences. The primary factor contributing to the overall rise in penetration rates is the reduced viscosity accompanying the temperature increase. The penetration rate of the three-layer combinations shows a decrease with rising temperature compared to superpositions. Specifically, at 20 °C, the three-layer combinations exhibit higher penetration rates, with no significant difference at 40 °C, and at 60 °C, superpositions display significantly higher penetration rates.

The three-layer combinations show an increase in the penetration rate by 17 %, 31 %, and 23 % for 40 °C and a decrease by 56 %, 53 %, and 54 % for 60 °C (A&S&A, C&S&C, and A&S&C) compared to the superposition of the penetration rates of the respective intra-material measurements. These trends are mirrored by the sizes of the inter-material and interfacial interaction effects (Fig. 5 b), indicating a decrease in multi-layer wetting with increasing temperature. The absolute values of the interaction effects for the cathode are greater than for the anode. The influence of the separator layer could be extracted by looking at the difference in the interaction effects I
A and I
A,A , according to Eqs. (6)–(8).

To further investigate the temperature sensitivity of the wetting properties, the measured wetting rates D are analyzed using the modified Arrhenius equations (1) and (2). These show a degressive-asymptotic relationship between wetting rate D and temperature T, which results from the temperature-related change in viscosity of the electrolyte. Activation energy is determined for all measurements from the fitted regression line of wetting rate over the reciprocal temperature 1/T (Fig. 6 ). The slope of the regression corresponds to the ratio of E
α
/R where R is the ideal gas constant R = 8.314 kJ (mol K)−1. For all nine measurement variants (see Fig. S7 Supplementary Material), the R2-values of the regression line exceed 0.95, indicating a robust Arrhenius relationship between the measured wetting rate D and the electrolyte temperature T.

The activation energies E
α,measured , representative of the energetic resistance during wetting, are displayed in Table 4 . Individual materials exhibit significantly higher activation energies than two-layer combinations, and the transition from two to three layers leads to a further substantial reduction in activation energy, highlighting attainable wetting improvement through inter-material and interface paths. The combined activation energies E
α,fit result from the following transition function:
(16)
E
α , f i t
= f
(
E
α 1
,
E
α 2
)
=
D 1
⋅
E
α 1
⋅
E
α 2
E
α 1
+
E
α 2
+
D 2

The constants D
1 = 0.8053 and D
2 = −1.6835 kJ mol−1 were computed by fitting the transition function to the values of the measured activation energies E
α,measured with an adjusted R2-value of 0.9557.

Due to the lower values of the three-layer interaction effect compared to the two-layer interaction effect, it can be concluded that there is a disproportionate increase in the intra-material wetting behavior of the separator with increasing temperature. This leads to the aforementioned decreases in the interaction effect. This temperature sensitivity may be attributed to the intra-material separator wetting, which is known for its sharp increase in penetration rates. This results in higher penetration rates for superpositions and negative inter-material and interfacial interaction effects. The heightened temperature sensitivity may be influenced by the holder, which disproportionately impacts separator wetting due to the interface between the separator and the holder surface.

Nevertheless, the penetration rate for all measurements (intra-material, two-layer combination, and three-layer combination) significantly increases across the three temperature levels. While the penetration rate is influenced by electrode properties, electrolyte properties, and the electrode/electrolyte interface, the increase is predominantly attributed to the reduction in viscosity, given that only electrolyte properties are genuinely temperature-dependent.

The functional relationship for determining the activation energy combinations corresponds to a parallel connection of electrical resistors. Consequently, the combination of multiple materials resembles a parallel connection of their activation energies. In this scenario, the electrolyte flow distributes among the individual materials based on the resistance of their activation energy, ensuring that the total activation energy remains smaller than the lowest individual activation energy. The introduced correction factors, D
1 and D
2 , supplement the original formula within the context of electrical circuit theory, potentially capturing the effects of the meniscus and the liquid lamella in the contact area of the ESC materials with the electrolyte.

In summary, increasing process temperatures according to the Arrhenius relationship results in higher penetration rates, accompanied by a decrease in the interaction effect. The correlation between the enhancement of wetting properties through the combination of ESC materials has also been established through the combination of activation energies and described by Eq. (16). As suggested in other publications, a wetting phase in an elevated temperature range is useful as long as the ESC materials or the electrolyte are not damaged.

The second row of Fig. 5 illustrates the penetration rate for intra-material separator measurements and three-layer combinations with varying separators (c) alongside the calculated sizes of the inter-material and interfacial interaction effects (d). Analyzing the observed intra-material measurement penetration rates reveal the following decreasing wettability order of separators:
S2 > S1 > S3 > S4 > S5,

with significant differences in mean values. This order corresponds to the ranking of the porosities of the separators. Even when comparing the normalized wetting rates the ranking remains the same (see Fig. 7 ).

In addition to the porosity, the observed differences can also be attributed to the materials. S1, S2 and S3 are based on nonwovens. S2, as a non-coated separator with the highest stated porosity, has the highest penetration rate (∼4–8 times as fast as S1 or S3). The penetration rates of S4 and S5, which are based on polyolefins exhibiting poor wettability with hydrocarbon-based electrolytes, are significantly lower than the non-woven-based separators. However, the order shifts for three-layer combinations:
S1 ∼ = S2 > S5 > S4 ∼ = S3 ≫ S5lam

Combinations with the non-woven-based S1 and S2 demonstrate the highest penetration rates, with no significant differences between the two separators. The lowest intra-material measurement penetration rate for S5 translates to its third-highest rate in the combination. This discrepancy is attributed to the PVDF coating, which enhances wettability [48,49]. The full contact surface with electrodes during combination measurements facilitates better electrolyte coupling into the separator's layer system. Conversely, the polyolefin-based S4 exhibit a poor penetration rate among unmodified separators, implying a need for increased entry areas for electrolytes in polyolefin-based separators for enhancing wettability compared to non-woven separators. Overall, mean penetration rates for three-layer combinations exhibit no significant differences, leading to the conclusion that the dominant wetting mechanism in this case is the interaction effect. Interaction effects are most effective when both electrodes are present. However, S5 experiences a significant deterioration in wettability when laminated onto electrodes, exhibiting a negative effect compared to all other separator combinations.

It can be concluded, that in absolute terms, the increase in wetting behavior (interaction effects) in relation to the superpositions for all separators is higher for the cathode than for the anode in the two-layer and three-layer combinations. This is due to the higher interaction effect of the cathode surfaces in the two-layer combination. Positive interaction effects for the electrode-separator wetting are observed for S1, S3, S4, and S5. The combination increases penetration rates compared to the superposition of individual measurements, with a greater effect on the anode because the increase between the interactions of the two-layer to the three-layer measurements is greater. Anode-separator inter-material exchange is more efficient than cathode-separator exchange, potentially attributed to the anode's surface structure and is having a higher thickness and therefore profiting more from inter-material wetting.

For S2 and S5lam, the interaction effect caused by the addition of the separator is negative, predominantly influencing the cathode-separator combination in the case of S2. The negative effect results from the exceptionally high intra-material wetting properties of S2, hindering the exploitation of its overall greater wetting potential.

Notably, S5lam exhibits an unusually high interaction effect size, suggesting an alteration of the PVDF layer or an excessive reduction of the interface through lamination, thereby inhibiting inter-material wetting paths and those along the interface. These results differ from those reported in previous investigations, where lamination significantly improved the wettability of the ESC [45]. However, the separator used there featured a PVDF layer in roughly 300 μm wide lines at regular intervals of around 900 μm, potentially benefitting wetting along the interface gap. In conclusion, the impact of ESC lamination on wetting ability depends on the specific products employed and therefore requires further investigation.

In summary, it can be stated that the intra-material wetting differences of the separators are mitigated in multi-layer wetting, making the wetting bottleneck of the separator less critical to the process. However, lamination in this material combination results in a deterioration of the wetting behavior. Therefore, it is essential to note for practical applications that the method of PVDF application significantly influences the wetting behavior.

The penetration rate of the two- and three-layer combinations for the differently calendered cathodes is shown in the fourth row of Fig. 5 on the left (e), along with the calculated inter-material and interfacial interaction effects on the right (f). The measurements for the corresponding combination only impact the anode and are not displayed here, as they were kept constant (see Fig. 4 for these measurements). The difference in calendering density of the cathodes leads to a decrease in the penetration rate with increasing calendering density from the uncalendered C2 to the calendered C3 (2.9 g cm−³) and to calendered C4 (3.2 g cm−³) for all measurements. This trend is observed in both the two- and three-layer combinations as well as intra-material probes and has already been investigated for intra-material wetting as reported by the studies of Sheng et al. and Davoodabadi et al. [20,52].

Due to these findings, calendering initially improves wettability but then reduces it due to a reduction in porosity and average pore diameter. The decrease in penetration rates for C&C and C&C&S is less pronounced than the reduction in effective layer thickness resulting from calendering – the multiplication of layer thickness and porosity, which is directly proportional to the wettable pore volume. Consequently, the wetting rates of the two calendered cathodes, C3 and C4, surpass that of the uncalendered cathode C2.

In the case of intra-material wetting and the three-layer combination A&C&S, the wetting rate diminishes with an escalating degree of calendering. Although the mean values of the calculated penetration rate interaction effect for the cathode don't show significant differences due to a high standard deviation (error propagation), their relational comparison indicates that the interaction effect is greater for the calendered C3 and C4 than for the uncalendered C2. This could be due to the worsened wetting properties of the cathode overall, leading to the exchange of electrolyte through the shorter inter-material wetting paths having a greater impact. The calendering process, which smoothens the cathode surface, might also contribute to improved wetting along the interface between the layers (see Fig. 8 ).

In summary, the penetration rate decrease with increasing calendering density consistently across both intra-material and inter-material setups The interaction effect is higher for calendered cathodes, likely due to improved electrolyte exchange along shorter inter-material wetting paths and a smoother cathode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of the product parameter “cathode material” (TS5)
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 5 (g), the comparison of penetration rates for different combinations is shown for two cathodes with different active materials. C3 is a cathode with NMC622 as the active material, while C5 consists of NMC811. The comparison shows that the difference in penetration rates for the single electrode setup is very small. However, C5 exhibits a slightly higher penetration rate, which is unexpected considering the similar porosity and density of the electrodes, as well as the smaller film thickness of C5.

In both the two-electrode and C&C&S setups, there is a notable surge in penetration rate. This surge is significant for the two-electrode setup and shows the same trend in the C&C&S setup, but with an overlapping standard deviation. This effect is much greater than the difference in the single-electrode setup and is also reflected in a difference in the interaction coefficient.

These observations lead to the hypothesis that for the NMC811 cathode, factors such as pore structure, wetting angle, or gap morphology enhance wettability. The interfacial gap between the electrodes seems to improve electrolyte wetting for the NMC811 cathode compared to the NMC622 cathode. Possible influencing factors include a beneficial pore structure, wetting angle, or gap morphology.

For example, the smaller layer thickness of the NMC811 cathode at the same area capacity likely leads to less binder segregation during drying, which could inhibit wetting in the thickness direction for the NMC622 cathode, explaining the greater extent of the interfacial wetting for the NMC811 cathode [50]. Another factor could be different surface roughnesses of the NMC622 and NMC811 cathodes, altering the gap morphology due to the NMC811 particles deforming and breaking less [51]. In the three-layer combination A&C&S, the penetration rate is not significantly different, presumably as a result of the inter-material compensation with the anode.

In summary, in comparison the interaction effect of the NMC811 material shows a significant higher rate, indicating that the interfacial gap improves electrolyte wetting for NMC811 more than for NMC622. This is likely due to differences in pore structure, reduced binder segregation, and surface roughness between the two cathode types. In practice, it is therefore necessary to check the wetting properties in the ESC if the electrode material changes or is mixed up, for which this methodology is suitable.

With these key findings from the five test series, summarized in Table 5 , the research gap for quantifying multi-layer wetting has been closed. The measurement and evaluation methodology proves to be robust, enabling the measurement of interfacial and inter-material wetting through the characteristic value of the interaction effect. This characteristic value can be practically utilized to obtain a meaningful quantification of wetting properties at the cell level through measurements at the material level. This approach is both cost- and time-efficient.

The relationships between wetting speed and process parameters such as temperature, as well as product parameters like the degree of calendaring, separator material, and cathode material, known from the literature, were confirmed and expanded to include the influence of the interaction effect.

The limitations of this study, despite the numerous tested materials and their combinations (cathode and separators), lie in the realization that the results are still highly material-dependent and, therefore, only valid for these specific materials. Additionally, the presented methodology does not distinguish between interfacial and inter-material wetting.

When considering the transferability of the measured ESC wetting at the material level, the absence of additional process influences (pressure, cell geometry) that occur during the wetting of battery cells must be taken into account.

In conclusion, this study has presented an experimental methodology designed to systematically quantify the interaction effects of the multi-layer wetting. The focus lies on understanding the interplay between electrodes and separators, examining the three different interaction effects during wetting. The investigations reveal a quantifiable interaction effect in a two- and three-layer material combination – consisting of pairs of electrodes as well as ESCs – which discerns to distinct anode and cathode interaction effects, maintaining consistency across diverse product and parameter variations.

The Arrhenius equation demonstrates a combinatorial relationship governing multi-layer wetting across various temperatures. The experiments include variations in product components (separators, cathodes) and parameter adjustments (electrolyte temperature, degree of calendering). As temperature increases, penetration rates rise, but the interaction effect declines.

Non-woven separators show enhanced penetration rates in multi-layer combinations compared to polyolefin-based separators. However, lamination impacts wetting based on separator type and structure. Contrary to previous findings, this study shows a significant decrease in wettability due to full-surface lamination of PVDF layers. Without lamination, the positive wetting effect of the PVDF coating is confirmed, especially in multi-layer measurements. NMC811 cathode combinations exhibit higher wetting rates than NMC622 combinations, likely due to greater surface roughness. However, this increase is mitigated when combined with anodes. Varying the degree of calendering results in a reduced penetration rate for cathodes, counteracted by inter-material wetting. This study's findings highlight the significant influence of material modifications when combined with other ESC components.

Moving forward, this approach provides a foundation for investigating additional product or process parameters, including multi-coated, structured, or post-dried electrodes, as well as new material combinations such as silicon-containing anodes or lithium iron phosphate cathodes.

The influence of compression on the measured materials should be analyzed in greater depth and systematically. Additionally, the scaling of the obtained material wetting parameters to large-format battery cells represents an intriguing direction for future exploration. The three-layer measurements provide an application-oriented material parameter for this scaling which in combination with product and process parameters (e.g. geometric cell dimensions, filling and evacuation pressure) might be combined to form dedicated models for wettability process predictions based on chosen ESC materials. In addition, the comparison with mercury intrusion measurements might be useful to further understand the wetting ESC components on the microstructure scale.

Nicolaj Kaden: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Do M. Nguyen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation. Ricarda Schlimbach: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis. Tobias Ohnimus: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources. Arno Kwade: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Klaus Dröder: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the support by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) for funding this research within the research project Cell-Fill (grant number 03XP0237A). Cell-Fill investigates different parameters influencing wetting, innovative filling processes and measurement methods, as well as simulation models for dispensing and wetting. A special thanks goes to Sören Scheffler for the contribution of the different calendered cathode material and the SEM-images of these cathodes. This material was produced in the project InteKal (grant number 03XP0348A) funded by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) for the InZePro competence cluster.
We acknowledge support by the Open Access Publication Funds of Technische Universität Braunschweig.

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2024.235282.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Interaction effect of the separator in an anode-separator-anode configuration
Interaction effect of the separator in an cathode-separator- cathode configuration
Interaction effect of the separator in an anode-separator- cathode configuration

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery Monitor 2023: the Value Chain between Economy and Ecology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kampker, H.H. Heimes, W. Bernhart, Battery Monitor 2023: The Value Chain Between Economy and Ecology, 3 rd edition,, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: REGULATION (EU) 2018/858 of the EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT and of the COUNCIL on the Approval and Market Surveillance of Motor Vehicles and Their Trailers, and of Systems, Components and Separate Technical Units Intended for Such Vehicles, Amending Regulations (EC) No 715/2007 and (EC) No 595/2009 and Repealing Directive 2007/46/EC: REGULATION (EU) 2018/858

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: European Union, REGULATION (EU) 2018/858 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL on the approval and market surveillance of motor vehicles and their trailers, and of systems, components and separate technical units intended for such vehicles, amending Regulations (EC) No 715/2007 and (EC) No 595/2009 and repealing Directive 2007/46/EC: REGULATION (EU) 2018/858, 2018.
J. Fleischmann, M. Hanicke, E. Horetsky, D. Ibrahim, S. Jautelat, M. Linder, P. Schaufuss, L. Torscht, A. van de Rijt, Battery 2030: Resilient, sustainable, and circular, 2023.
B. Scrosati, J. Garche, Journal of Power Sources 195 (2010) 2419-2430. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2009.11.048.
Y. Wang, H. Li, P. He, E. Hosono, H. Zhou, Nanoscale 2 (2010) 1294-1305. https://doi.org/10.1039/c0nr00068j.
A. Kwade, W. Haselrieder, R. Leithoff, A. Modlinger, F. Dietrich, K. Droeder, Nat Energy 3 (2018) 290-300. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41560-018-0130-3.
D.L. Wood, J. Li, C. Daniel, Journal of Power Sources 275 (2015) 234-242. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2014.11.019.
F. Duffner, N. Kronemeyer, J. Tubke, J. Leker, M. Winter, R. Schmuch, Nat Energy 6 (2021) 123-134. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41560-020-00748-8.
Y. Liu, R. Zhang, J. Wang, Y. Wang, iScience 24 (2021) 102332. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2021.102332.
N. Kaden, R. Schlimbach, A. Rohde Garcia, K. Droder, Batteries 9 (2023) 164. https://doi.org/10.3390/batteries9030164.
W. Pfleging, J. Proll, Journal of Materials Chemistry A 2 (2014) 14918-14926. https://doi.org/10.1039/c4ta02353f.
J. Hagemeister, S. Stock, M. Linke, M. Fischer, R. Drees, M. Kurrat, R. Daub, Energy Tech 11 (2023). https://doi.org/10.1002/ente.202200686.
D. Jang, S. Suh, H. Yoon, J. Kim, H. Kim, J. Baek, H.-J. Kim, Applied Surface Science Advances 6 (2021) 100168. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsadv.2021.100168.
M.-Y. An, H.-T. Kim, D.-R. Chang, Journal of Solid State Electrochemistry 18 (2014) 1807-1814. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10008-014-2412-4.
J. Fang, A. Kelarakis, Y.-W. Lin, E. Giannelis, L.-D. Tsai, ECS Meeting Abstracts MA2011-02 (2011) 1456. https://doi.org/10.1149/MA2011-02/17/1456.
C. Man, P. Jiang, K. Wong, Y. Zhao, C. Tang, M. Fan, W. Lau, J. Mei, S. Li, H. Liu, D. Hui, Journal of Materials Chemistry A 2 (2014) 11980-11986. https://doi.org/10.1039/c4ta01870b.
